title: Interfaces
description: A pure contract of behavior, solving Java's single-inheritance limit from the Inheritance lesson, plus default and static interface methods.
objectives: Understand an interface as a contract of method signatures
Implement an interface with implements, and implement multiple interfaces at once
Use default and static methods inside an interface

sections.text: In Inheritance, we noted a Java class can only extend ONE superclass. But what if Duck needs to be both Swimmer and Flyer, and Fish needs to be just Swimmer? These aren't a single-parent hierarchy -- they're independent CAPABILITIES. Interfaces express exactly this.
A contract that declares method signatures (and constants) without providing bodies for most of them, which any implementing class must fulfill using the implements keyword.

Every method inside an interface (like swim()) has NO body by default -- it's implicitly abstract, similar to an abstract method from the Abstract Classes lesson, but you don't even write the word abstract; it's assumed. Fish must implement swim() with public visibility -- interface methods are implicitly public, so overriding them requires public, not less.

sections.output:
The duck swims.
The duck flies.

sections.text: This is exactly what Inheritance's single-superclass limit couldn't do -- Duck implements BOTH Swimmer and Flyer at once, comma-separated. A class can implement as many interfaces as it needs, even though it can still extend only one class.

Modern Java lets an interface provide an actual, shared implementation for a method, using default -- implementing classes get this behavior automatically, without being forced to write it themselves.

Person never wrote greet() itself -- it comes free from Greeter's default implementation, which calls getName() (which Person DID implement) via polymorphism, the same runtime behavior covered in the Polymorphism lesson.

A static method inside an interface behaves like the static methods from Static vs Instance Methods -- called directly on the interface name, not on any implementing object, and it's not inherited by implementing classes the way default methods are.

sections.title: Interface vs abstract class
rows: A class can have how many?
default/static methods can have bodies; the rest cannot
Any mix of abstract and fully-implemented methods
Defining a capability/contract shared across unrelated classes
Sharing common code among closely related subclasses

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Forgetting public when implementing an interface method -- interface methods are implicitly public, and an overriding method cannot reduce that visibility.
Assuming an interface can only ever have abstract methods -- default and static methods are allowed to have real bodies.
Trying to extend multiple classes instead of realizing multiple interfaces is the correct Java tool for that need.
Confusing default methods (inherited automatically by implementing classes) with static methods (called directly on the interface, never inherited).

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is an interface, and how is it different from a class?
questions.answer: An interface is a contract of method signatures (mostly without bodies) that implementing classes must fulfill, declared with implements. Unlike a class, most interface methods have no implementation, and a class can implement any number of interfaces, unlike single-class inheritance.

questions.question: How does Java handle the lack of multiple inheritance using interfaces?
questions.answer: A class can implement multiple interfaces at once, even though it can only extend one superclass -- this lets a class combine multiple independent capabilities (like Swimmer and Flyer) without needing multiple parent classes.

questions.question: What is a default method in an interface?
questions.answer: A method inside an interface that provides an actual, shared implementation, which implementing classes inherit automatically without being forced to write it themselves -- unlike the interface's other, body-less method signatures.

questions.question: Can an interface have static methods? How are they called?
questions.answer: Yes. Interface static methods have a real body and are called directly on the interface name, like InterfaceName.methodName(), and are not inherited by implementing classes.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: An interface is a contract of method signatures that implementing classes fulfill using implements -- most methods have no body and are implicitly public and abstract. Unlike single-inheritance classes, a class can implement any number of interfaces, solving the need for multiple independent capabilities like Swimmer and Flyer on the same class. Modern interfaces also support default methods, which provide a real, shared implementation inherited automatically by implementing classes, and static methods, called directly on the interface and never inherited. Interfaces suit unrelated classes sharing a capability; abstract classes suit closely related subclasses sharing common code.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: interface = a CONTRACT of capabilities, not a family tree -- implement as MANY as you need. default = free behavior baked into the contract. static = a plain utility method living on the contract itself.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: How many interfaces can a single class implement?
What is a default method, and why is it useful?
How do you call a static method declared inside an interface?
When would you choose an interface over an abstract class?

codeExamples.output:
The duck swims.
The duck flies.
